verses.text: It came to pass in the days when the judges ruled, that there was a famine in the land. A certain man of Beth-lehem-judah went to sojourn in the country of Moab, he, and his wife, and his two sons. The name of the man was Elimelech, and the name of his wife Naomi, and the name of his two sons Mahlon and Chilion, Ephrathites of Beth-lehem-judah. They came into the country of Moab, and continued there. Guidance: 'In the days when the judges ruled' — the book opens by placing itself inside the era of moral collapse the previous book just chronicled. The name 'Bethlehem' means 'house of bread,' and the first crisis is that the house of bread is out of bread. A man named Elimelech ('my God is king') takes his family to Moab — to the same nation that hired Balaam to curse Israel and tempted them at Peor. The names of the two sons, Mahlon ('sickly') and Chilion ('failing'), seem to foreshadow the disaster. The book begins with covenant people fleeing covenant land to seek bread among an idolatrous people. Famine often forces choices that test whether God or geography is the source of provision.

It came to pass in the days when the judges ruled, that there was a famine in the land. A certain man of Beth-lehem-judah went to sojourn in the country of Moab, he, and his wife, and his two sons. The name of the man was Elimelech, and the name of his wife Naomi, and the name of his two sons Mahlon and Chilion, Ephrathites of Beth-lehem-judah. They came into the country of Moab, and continued there. Guidance: 'In the days when the judges ruled' — the book opens by placing itself inside the era of moral collapse the previous book just chronicled. The name 'Bethlehem' means 'house of bread,' and the first crisis is that the house of bread is out of bread. A man named Elimelech ('my God is king') takes his family to Moab — to the same nation that hired Balaam to curse Israel and tempted them at Peor. The names of the two sons, Mahlon ('sickly') and Chilion ('failing'), seem to foreshadow the disaster. The book begins with covenant people fleeing covenant land to seek bread among an idolatrous people. Famine often forces choices that test whether God or geography is the source of provision.

Elimelech Naomi's husband died; and she was left, and her two sons. They took them wives of the women of Moab; the name of the one was Orpah, and the name of the other Ruth: and they dwelled there about ten years. Mahlon and Chilion died also both of them; and the woman was left of her two sons and her husband. Guidance: Four funerals in three sentences. Elimelech first; ten years of marriage to Moabite wives; then both sons. Naomi loses her husband, then both her sons. Three Hebrew names crossed out, two Moabite names introduced — Orpah ('neck') and Ruth (possibly 'friend' or 'refreshment'). The narrator gives no commentary on whether the intermarriage was right (Deut 23:3 said Moabites should not enter the assembly to the tenth generation). The book is honest about Naomi's circumstances and refuses to moralize them prematurely. She is alone, foreign, widowed, childless. The story begins where most people's stories quietly end.

Then she arose with her daughters in law, that she might return from the country of Moab: for she had heard in the country of Moab how that the LORD had visited his people in giving them bread. Wherefore she went forth out of the place where she was, and her two daughters in law with her; and they went on the way to return unto the land of Judah. Guidance: 'The LORD had visited his people in giving them bread.' The famine in Bethlehem is over. The verb 'visited' is the word God uses when He intervenes for His people. Naomi hears the news in Moab and turns toward home. Notice the verbs: 'she arose,' 'she went forth,' 'they went on the way.' Movement begins where grief had settled. Sometimes the smallest news of God's renewed provision is what gets the widow off the bench.

Then she arose with her daughters in law, that she might return from the country of Moab: for she had heard in the country of Moab how that the LORD had visited his people in giving them bread. Wherefore she went forth out of the place where she was, and her two daughters in law with her; and they went on the way to return unto the land of Judah. Guidance: 'The LORD had visited his people in giving them bread.' The famine in Bethlehem is over. The verb 'visited' is the word God uses when He intervenes for His people. Naomi hears the news in Moab and turns toward home. Notice the verbs: 'she arose,' 'she went forth,' 'they went on the way.' Movement begins where grief had settled. Sometimes the smallest news of God's renewed provision is what gets the widow off the bench.

Naomi said unto her two daughters in law, Go, return each to her mother's house: the LORD deal kindly with you, as ye have dealt with the dead, and with me. The LORD grant you that ye may find rest, each of you in the house of her husband. Then she kissed them; and they lifted up their voice, and wept. Guidance: Naomi's prayer for her widowed daughters-in-law is striking — she asks the LORD (not Chemosh, their god) to bless them with Moabite husbands. She tells them to go to their mothers' houses (interestingly, not their fathers' — perhaps the mothers' households are where remarriage arrangements would be made). Her blessing is the chapter's first sermon: she begs God's kindness on women she could justly resent. Grief that refuses bitterness is its own ministry. The Hebrew word for 'kindly' is hesed — the loyal-love that runs through the whole book.

The LORD grant you that ye may find rest, each of you in the house of her husband. Then she kissed them; and they lifted up their voice, and wept. She tells them to go to their mothers' houses (interestingly, not their fathers' — perhaps the mothers' households are where remarriage arrangements would be made). They said unto her, Surely we will return with thee unto thy people. Naomi said, Turn again, my daughters: why will ye go with me? are there yet any more sons in my womb, that they may be your husbands? Turn again, my daughters, go your way; for I am too old to have an husband. If I should say, I have hope, if I should have an husband also to night, and should also bear sons; would ye tarry for them till they were grown? would ye stay for them from having husbands? nay, my daughters; for it grieveth me much for your sakes that the hand of the LORD is gone out against me. Guidance: Naomi names her bitterness in the open: 'the hand of the LORD is gone out against me.' She believes God has personally targeted her household. She is not lying about her feelings — but she may be misreading her circumstances. The text will eventually reveal the LORD's hand was preparing the great-grandmother of King David. In real time, all she can see is loss. Her logic about remarriage and levirate sons is the law of Deuteronomy 25 — but in her case impossible. She tells the truth, even when the truth seems to indict God. Honest faith does not pretend His hand is invisible when it feels heavy.

They lifted up their voice, and wept again: and Orpah kissed her mother in law; but Ruth clave unto her. Guidance: One verb each for two destinies. Orpah 'kissed' (a farewell). Ruth 'clave' (the same Hebrew verb used in Gen 2:24 for marriage — clinging like one flesh). Orpah is not portrayed as the villain — she did the sensible thing, the thing Naomi asked her to do. Ruth's is the unusual choice. Two women face the same crossroads and walk two different directions. Scripture honors them both and tells the story of only one. Sometimes faith is the difference between a kiss goodbye and a refusal to let go.

She said, Behold, thy sister in law is gone back unto her people, and unto her gods: return thou after thy sister in law. Ruth said, Intreat me not to leave thee, or to return from following after thee: for whither thou goest, I will go; and where thou lodgest, I will lodge: thy people shall be my people, and thy God my God: where thou diest, will I die, and there will I be buried: the LORD do so to me, and more also, if ought but death part thee and me. When she saw that she was stedfastly minded to go with her, then she left speaking unto her. Guidance: Naomi gives Ruth one last out: 'her gods' are mentioned — go back to the religion of Moab. Ruth's reply is one of the most beautiful confessions in scripture, often read at weddings but actually spoken to a mother-in-law. Five things she pledges: where you go, where you lodge, your people, your God, where you die. The last is the deepest — she chooses to be buried in Israelite soil, foreigner that she is. 'Thy God my God' is her conversion. She invokes 'the LORD' by name, swearing as a covenant member. The narrator's note 'she left speaking unto her' tells us Naomi recognized the conversion was real and quit arguing. Some pledges silence even a grief-broken older woman. Ruth becomes a model of Gentile faith — Boaz will later call her 'a virtuous woman' (3:11), the same phrase Proverbs 31 uses.

So they two went until they came to Beth-lehem. It came to pass, when they were come to Beth-lehem, that all the city was moved about them, and they said, Is this Naomi? She said unto them, Call me not Naomi, call me Mara: for the Almighty hath dealt very bitterly with me. I went out full, and the LORD hath brought me home again empty: why then call ye me Naomi, seeing the LORD hath testified against me, and the Almighty hath afflicted me? Guidance: 'All the city was moved' — the whole town turns out for the homecoming. The women remember her. But her response is one of scripture's most honest laments. 'Naomi' means 'pleasant'; she demands the new name 'Mara' — 'bitter.' She uses two of God's names — Shaddai ('Almighty,' the patriarchal name of provision) and YHWH (the LORD) — to charge Him with her ruin. Her theology is not wrong; God does indeed govern the loss. Her interpretation is partial: she calls herself empty in the very verse where Ruth stands beside her, soon to be the great-grandmother of David. The reader sees what she cannot. Sometimes the LORD's deepest mercies are walking next to us while we feel emptiest.

So Naomi returned, and Ruth the Moabitess, her daughter in law, with her, which returned out of the country of Moab: and they came to Beth-lehem in the beginning of barley harvest. Guidance: The chapter's closing sentence carries the seed of the next. They arrive 'in the beginning of barley harvest' — about April. The famine is over, the fields are gold. Naomi is empty in her own eyes; the providence of God is already plotting wheat and a kinsman-redeemer in the rows ahead. The widow's calendar and the LORD's calendar are about to meet, and the meeting will happen in a field.
